items.title: Bond sell-off + gold drop = global liquidity sliding — Phibro-Salomon as the historical template
items.idea: Capital Wars invokes the 1980s Phibro-Salomon implosion as the historical parallel: commodities up = bonds down, so the marriage made sense — until Volcker killed inflation and bond markets dominated the next decade. The current bond sell-off combined with a drop in gold are framed as warning signs that global liquidity is sliding, echoing the regime-change dynamics of the early-Volcker era. When both bonds AND gold sell simultaneously, the liquidity contraction signal is unusually clean.
items.angle: The Phibro-Salomon parallel cuts both ways: if liquidity is sliding, commodities face headwinds alongside risk assets. The bond/gold co-move is the chart to watch this week.
items.tweetA: Bond sell-off AND gold dropping together. That's not normal. Usually one hedges the other. When both fall, it's a liquidity signal — money is leaving, not rotating. Capital Wars invokes Phibro-Salomon: the 1980s told us what a real regime change looks like. We might be in one.
items.tweetB: The Phibro-Salomon merger in 1981 was built on an inverse correlation: commodities up when bonds down. Volcker destroyed that thesis. Today's co-move of bond sell-off + gold drop suggests the liquidity regime is shifting again. The question isn't which asset to own — it's whether any asset wants to be owned right now.

items.title: US crypto clarity bill passed — the 2-year regulatory overhang is gone
items.idea: The US crypto clarity bill has passed, removing the core regulatory overhang that kept institutional capital on the sidelines for two years. This is the single most significant US regulatory event for crypto since the ETF approvals. Markets had priced in the uncertainty discount for long enough that the passage itself is now the test: do buyers show up, or was regulatory clarity never the real reason they were absent?
items.angle: If crypto can't rally meaningfully on this, the positioning signal is bearish — the excuse is gone and buyers aren't showing up. Watch BTC and ETH price action in the sessions after passage.
items.tweetA: US crypto clarity bill passed. Two years of 'we're waiting for regulatory clarity' just ran out of excuses. Either institutional capital shows up now or we learn that clarity was never the real reason they were waiting.
items.tweetB: Crypto regulatory clarity: done. The 2-year overhang is gone. This is the setup everyone said they were waiting for. The next 30 days are going to tell us whether that was true or whether the real reason was price.

items.title: Japanese investors dumping US bonds — largest foreign holder actively selling at the worst moment
items.idea: Japanese investors are dumping US Treasuries — not slowing purchases, actively selling. Japan is the largest foreign holder of US debt. This is happening simultaneously with Kevin Warsh taking the Fed chair hawkish, the US deficit at record levels, and domestic US yield pressure already building. Yen weakness + domestic JGB yield normalization + trade friction = a seller that cannot be QE'd away.
items.angle: Japan selling + Warsh hawkish + record deficit + foreign demand withdrawal = structural 10Y pressure that the Fed cannot directly address. This is the macro trade of the next 6 months if selling continues.
items.tweetA: Japan is selling US Treasuries. Not slowing purchases — selling. The largest foreign holder of US debt is reducing exposure precisely when the new Fed chair is hawkish and the deficit is at record levels. The bond market math is getting uncomfortable.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching the Fed. Nobody's modeling Japan. Yen weakness + domestic JGB yield normalization + trade tension = a forced seller that doesn't care what Warsh says. US 10Y yields have a Japan problem that no amount of Fed communication fixes.

items.title: Coinbase deploys USDC natively on Hyperliquid — largest US exchange backs the dominant on-chain perps venue
items.idea: Coinbase has deployed USDC as the native stablecoin on Hyperliquid, the leading on-chain perpetuals DEX. This is the largest US regulated exchange backing the dominant on-chain derivatives venue. It legitimizes Hyperliquid as institutional-grade and gives Coinbase a revenue stake in on-chain derivatives volume — a market they cannot fully capture on their own CEX. This is Coinbase hedging their own CEX obsolescence.
items.angle: When USDC is native on Hyperliquid, settlement risk drops and institutional capital can flow on-chain. Watch Hyperliquid TVL and volume for the institutional uptick. The CEX/DEX line keeps blurring, and Coinbase is on both sides of it.
items.tweetA: Coinbase deployed USDC natively on Hyperliquid. Translation: the largest US crypto exchange just backed the most dangerous competitor to centralized perps trading. They're not fighting on-chain derivatives — they're funding them.
items.tweetB: USDC on Hyperliquid is bigger than it sounds. Coinbase is now the liquidity backbone of the hottest on-chain derivatives venue. Native stablecoin means lower settlement risk and easier institutional flow. The CEX/DEX line keeps blurring — and Coinbase is deliberately on both sides.

items.source: Big Moves / Big Price Moves Explained
items.title: Bubble warnings from practitioners who have seen it before — Dillian and Peters
items.idea: Jared Dillian (The Daily Dirtnap), who was on the trading floor at the top of the dot-com bubble: 'Too many idiots making too much money — pretty much the definition of a bubble.' Eric Peters (One River Asset Management): 'investors who build nothing of substance make lots of money for a period by applying reckless leverage to fairly mundane investments.' These are not tourist permabears. The Peters framing maps precisely to the private credit/CLO/carry trade complex.
items.angle: The most reliable bubble indicator isn't a PE ratio — it's identifying who you respect least who is making 3x what you do and listening to how they explain it. The social signal is the tell.
items.tweetA: Jared Dillian at 26 watched the dot-com from the trading floor: 'too many idiots making too much money — that's the definition of a bubble.' Eric Peters: 'reckless leverage to fairly mundane investments.' Neither is a permabear. They've seen it. They're saying they're seeing it again.
items.tweetB: The most reliable bubble signal isn't a PE ratio. It's identifying who you respect least in the business who is making 3x what you do and listening to how they explain it. If that sentence makes you think of specific people right now — the signal is live.

items.source: Big Moves / Big Price Moves Explained
items.title: SolarEdge +22.9%: gross margins 8%→23.5% in one year, analyst consensus $30 vs $61 stock, pivoting to AI data center power via SiC solid-state transformers
items.idea: SolarEdge Q1 2026: gross margin 23.5% (non-GAAP) vs 8.0% a year ago — sixth consecutive quarter of expansion. Near breakeven operating profitability expected by Q2 midpoint. Revenue $310.5M, +46% YoY. Despite execution, 24 analysts hold an average price target of $30.40 vs ~$61 trading price, implying 43.8% downside. Management is pivoting to AI data center power via silicon carbide solid-state transformers — 'multi-billion dollar TAM outside of traditional solar' targeting 2027-28 pipeline.
items.angle: The street is aggressively short while the company executes. The SiC data center angle is not in any analyst model at $30.40. When forced to revise, the consensus move will be violent. $SEDG
items.tweetA: SolarEdge gross margin: 8% → 23.5% in twelve months. Near breakeven in Q2. Stock rips 22%. And 24 sell-side analysts still have an average $30.40 target on a $61 stock — 43% implied downside. The street is fighting the tape and losing. $SEDG
items.tweetB: Buried in the SolarEdge quarter: solid-state transformers using silicon carbide for AI data centers. 'Multi-billion dollar TAM outside solar' by 2027-28. The $30 consensus is modeling a solar company. The $61 stock is pricing a power infrastructure company with AI optionality. $SEDG

items.source: Big Moves / Big Price Moves Explained
items.title: Enphase +10.2%: solar ITC demand pull-forward ahead of July deadline — Q3 hangover not priced in
items.idea: Enphase rallied on preorders for its IQ9S-3P commercial microinverter, letting customers lock in the 30% federal solar tax credit ahead of the July deadline. The entire residential solar segment rallied in tandem with SolarEdge. This is a pull-forward dynamic: Q3 demand is being borrowed to capture expiring incentives. The post-deadline cliff is not priced in — installer channel inventories will become the tell in July.
items.angle: Buy the ITC deadline rally into solar, then short the air pocket after. The Q3 residential solar setup will be a volume vacuum. The risk is Congressional extension — watch for any legislative signals on ITC renewal. $ENPH $SEDG
items.tweetA: Enphase surges on solar preorders ahead of the July ITC deadline. This is demand pull-forward, not structural demand creation. You're borrowing from Q3 to lock in Q2 numbers. When the deadline passes and the backlog is empty, watch what happens to installation run rates. $ENPH
items.tweetB: $ENPH +10% on ITC deadline rush. The 30% federal solar tax credit expires July. Everyone is pulling forward. The trade: solar stocks into the deadline, then short the air pocket after. Setup is clean. Only risk is Congress extending it again — which given current politics, is not zero.

items.source: Big Moves / Big Price Moves Explained
items.title: DexCom Investor Day: 10%+ annual revenue growth through 2030, 37% EBITDA margin target, $1B buyback — Elliott at the table, RBC says targets look conservative
items.idea: DexCom outlined 2030 targets: 10%+ annual revenue growth, 30% operating margin, 37% EBITDA margin, $1B share repurchase. Elliott Management involved with governance changes. RBC stated targets appear conservative with potential upside beyond. When an activist is in the room and management sets multi-year guides, the targets are designed to be beaten. The GLP-1/CGM intersection adds upside: people on Ozempic becoming hyperaware of metabolic health may drive CGM adoption rather than reduce it.
items.angle: DexCom's 2030 targets don't need the GLP-1 tailwind to work — but if GLP-1 adoption drives metabolic monitoring demand instead of killing it, 10% annual growth looks very conservative. Elliott plus conservative guidance equals a floor, not a ceiling. $DXCM
items.tweetA: DexCom Investor Day: 10%+ annual revenue growth, 37% EBITDA margin by 2030, $1B buyback, Elliott at the table. RBC says the targets look conservative. When an activist is in the room and management guides conservatively — that's not guidance, that's a floor. $DXCM
items.tweetB: The GLP-1 bear case on CGM: Ozempic kills glucose monitoring demand. The bull case: everyone on GLP-1s becomes hyperaware of metabolic health and wants continuous monitoring. DexCom's 2030 targets don't need that thesis — but if it plays out, 10% annual growth is massively wrong. $DXCM

items.source: Big Moves / Big Price Moves Explained
items.title: Nu Holdings -5.7%: expected credit losses $1.72B, adjusted EBIT misses by 32% — EM consumer credit cycle warning
items.idea: Nu Holdings Q1 2026: adjusted EBIT $1.04B vs $1.52B consensus — a 32% miss. Expected credit losses rose to $1.72B, up sharply year-over-year. Risk-adjusted NIM compressed and gross profit declined sequentially. The Brazilian fintech darling expanded aggressively into lower-income demographics and is now absorbing the credit cycle: rapid expansion at scale eventually meets delinquency normalization. This is not company-specific — it signals the EM consumer credit cycle is turning.
items.angle: The NU miss maps to a broader EM fintech credit cycle. Watch Nubank peers in LatAm and India for similar dynamics. The credit expansion phase is reversing. $NU
items.tweetA: Nu Holdings: adjusted EBIT $1.04B vs $1.52B expected. Expected credit losses hit $1.72B, up sharply YoY. You can grow revenue faster than any bank in history — but credit losses are mean-reverting. Brazil is teaching Nu the oldest banking lesson. $NU
items.tweetB: The NU miss is not a company-specific story. It's the EM consumer credit cycle. Rapid fintech expansion into unbanked demographics works until default rates normalize. $1.72B in expected credit losses at Q1 says the normalization has started. Watch EM fintech broadly. $NU

items.source: Big Moves / Big Price Moves Explained
items.title: LKQ securities class actions: $2B+ Uni-Select deal called 'minimal integration risk' while FinishMaster was already losing customers — CEO departed
items.idea: LKQ paid $2B+ for Uni-Select/FinishMaster (Feb 2023), describing it as 'compelling strategic fit with minimal integration risk.' Multiple securities class actions allege FinishMaster was already losing major customers at announcement. North American segment missed quarterly revenue by ~$200M and EBITDA margins compressed by high single digits YoY. CEO Dominick Zarcone departed post-disclosure. The litigation overhang will constrain capital allocation and management bandwidth for years.
items.angle: The LKQ template is instructive for any large M&A narrative right now: 'minimal integration risk' is the tell. The disclosure failure pattern will suppress LKQ's multiple regardless of operational repair — management credibility is the scarcest capital in corporate finance. $LKQ
items.tweetA: $2.1B deal. Called 'minimal integration risk.' FinishMaster was already losing customers. Now LKQ has multiple securities class actions, a departed CEO, and stock near 52-week lows. The sequence from 'compelling strategic fit' to shareholder litigation took 36 months. $LKQ
items.tweetB: LKQ's real problem isn't the lawsuits — it's that every future management statement now gets discounted by 'you said that about FinishMaster.' Credibility is the scarcest capital in corporate finance. They spent all of it on one bad acquisition narrative. $LKQ

items.source: Big Moves / Big Price Moves Explained
items.title: AEP -2.7%: 23.5M share forward sale — utilities fund AI power capex with your equity dilution
items.idea: American Electric Power disclosed forward sale agreements for ~23.5 million shares (~2.5% dilution). Utilities issue equity when the stock price is relatively high and they need capital for large capex programs. AEP is funding the AI data center infrastructure buildout. Every megawatt of new load that justifies the capex also creates the rationale for dilutive equity issuance. The AI power demand thesis is real — but utilities are telling you how the economics work: shareholders fund the infrastructure.
items.angle: Don't confuse a good sector thesis (AI power demand) with a good stock setup (dilutive regulated capex). The load growth is real, returns are regulated, shareholders keep getting asked to fund it. $AEP
items.tweetA: American Electric Power: 23.5 million shares in forward sale agreements. The AI power demand story is real. But utilities fund that capex with your equity. Every new data center megawatt = more share dilution. $AEP is showing you how the economics work — you're paying for the infrastructure. $AEP
items.tweetB: Utilities at all-time highs are issuing equity to fund AI data center infrastructure. That's not a bug — it's the feature. Load growth is real, returns are regulated, shareholders foot the bill. Don't confuse a good sector thesis with a good stock setup. $AEP

items.title: Intelligence is not power: automated firms outcompete via capitalist dynamics, not ASI takeover — Garett Jones national IQ spillovers apply
items.idea: Dwarkesh argues that defining intelligence as 'ability to achieve goals across domains' would make Stalin the most intelligent person who ever lived — revealing the definition is actually about power, not intelligence. Real-world power derives from authority and trust (Trump is powerful because the government gives him authority, not because he outthinks everyone). AI systems improving at coding/economic tasks are not strongly correlated with power-seeking. Garett Jones shows individual IQ only modestly predicts income, but national IQ strongly predicts national outcomes — intelligence has group-level spillover effects.
items.angle: This directly undermines the paperclip-maximizer ASI takeover framing. The real disruption is automated firms outcompeting via normal capitalist dynamics — ecosystem wide, not single-actor. Bet on AI infrastructure and platforms, not on moats around individual models.
items.tweetA: By the standard AI risk definition — 'achieves goals across domains' — Stalin was more intelligent than Einstein. Dwarkesh's point: power comes from authority and trust, not galaxy-brain scheming. The physicists don't run the world. This breaks most ASI takeover narratives at the root.
items.tweetB: Garett Jones: individual IQ modestly predicts individual income, national IQ strongly predicts national outcomes. Intelligence has spillover effects at the group level, not the individual. The same logic applies to AI: automated firms outcompeting via normal capitalist dynamics is how this unfolds — not one AI outscheming humanity.

items.title: Fed chair transition base rates: median 13% drawdown in year one — Greenspan took the chair 11 weeks before Black Monday
items.idea: Across 15 Fed chairs since 1916, median 6-month return after handover is +2.4% and median 1-year is +10.6% — respectable on the surface. But the drawdown distribution is the story: median peak-to-trough inside year 1 is ~13%, average ~17%, dragged by chairs who walked in before the wheels came off (Meyer into the Depression, Greenspan 11 weeks before Black Monday, Burns into the early-70s bear). Markets also tend to rally INTO appointments — Powell had a +14.3% six-month run-up before taking the chair, then corrected.
items.angle: S&P at ATH into Warsh mirrors the exact historical pattern. The base rate says the test comes within 6 months; June 16-17 FOMC is the first candidate. Size equity risk accordingly.
items.tweetA: 15 Fed chairs since 1916. Median year-1 return: +10.6%. Sounds fine. Median peak-to-trough drawdown inside year 1: 13%. Average: 17%. Greenspan took the chair 11 weeks before Black Monday. The median conceals the distribution — and we're in the ATH setup that historically precedes the test.
items.tweetB: Markets rally INTO new Fed chair appointments, then test the new chair. Powell had a +14.3% run-up before taking the chair. S&P is at ATH into Warsh. The historical base rate for year-1 drawdowns is 13-17%. That's not a forecast — it's a prior worth carrying when you size equity risk over the next 6 months.

items.title: Warsh confirmed 54-45 (most partisan ever), CPI at 3-year high post US-Israel-Iran energy shock, 4 FOMC dissents in April — June 16-17 is genuine event risk
items.idea: Kevin Warsh confirmed 54-45 — the most partisan Fed chair vote in history; only Fetterman crossed from Democrats. He inherits: S&P at all-time highs, CPI at 3-year high following the energy shock from the US-Israel-Iran conflict, 4 FOMC dissents in April (most divided since 1992), Trump openly demanding cuts. Warsh's stated preferences — faster balance-sheet runoff, tighter inflation discipline — are hawkish relative to where Trump needs policy to land. June 16-17 is the first FOMC under Warsh and is a genuine event-risk catalyst.
items.angle: The political dynamics make a rate cut before Q4 nearly impossible for Warsh without destroying his inflation credibility on day one. Bond market and equity market will have very different views on June 16-17. Long vol into that meeting is cheap relative to the setup.
items.tweetA: Warsh inherits: CPI at 3-year high post Iran energy shock, 4 FOMC dissents in April (most since 1992), S&P at ATH, Trump demanding cuts. Warsh's preferences: faster QT, tighter inflation discipline. Something has to give at June 16-17 FOMC. Long vol.
items.tweetB: Most partisan Fed confirmation in history: 54-45, one Democrat crossed. No political cover. If Warsh cuts, the bond market screams inflation capitulation. If he holds, Trump screams political interference. There's no clean exit from June 16-17 — and it's 4 weeks away.

items.title: Nous Research Token Superposition Training: 2-3x wall-clock speedup, 61% compute reduction on 10B MoE — zero architecture change at inference
items.idea: Token Superposition Training (TST) modifies only the pretraining loop — no architecture change, optimizer, tokenizer, or training data changes. During the first third of training, the model reads/predicts contiguous token bags by averaging embeddings; the rest is standard next-token prediction. The inference model is identical to conventionally pretrained output. Validated at 270M, 600M, 3B dense, and 10B-A1B MoE. The 10B MoE trained in 4,768 B200-GPU-hours vs baseline 12,311 — 61% compute reduction. Second pretraining-loop efficiency paper from Nous this week alongside Lighthouse Attention.
items.angle: If TST replicates at 70B+ scale, training compute costs drop 50-60% across the industry. That compresses the cost moat of large labs and accelerates open-source models catching closed-source capabilities. Watch for Llama and Mistral teams to adopt this.
items.tweetA: Nous Research: Token Superposition Training delivers 2-3x wall-clock speedup at matched FLOPs. The 10B MoE trained in 4,768 B200-GPU-hours vs 12,311 for baseline — 61% compute reduction. Zero architecture change; the deployed model is identical. If this holds at 70B+, training cost moats just thinned considerably.
items.tweetB: Two pretraining efficiency papers from the same lab this week: Lighthouse Attention (21x faster at 512K context) + Token Superposition Training (2-3x wall-clock speedup, 61% compute reduction at 10B MoE). Nous Research is moving faster on training efficiency than any of the big labs. Neither changes the deployed model.

items.title: Lighthouse Attention (Nous Research): 21x faster forward pass at 512K context — training-only wrapper, vanilla attention at inference
items.idea: Nous Research's Lighthouse Attention wraps standard SDPA with a hierarchical gradient-free selection layer that compresses/decompresses queries, keys, and values symmetrically while preserving causality. The wrapper is removed near end of training in a short recovery phase. Deployed model runs vanilla attention — zero inference overhead. Reports 21x faster forward latency at 512K context and lower final loss than full-attention baselines under matched FLOPs.
items.angle: The deployment-free efficiency gain is the killer feature. Most efficient-attention methods require changing the inference stack — this one doesn't. The 21x at 512K context is directly relevant to long-document and agentic workloads where context length is the binding constraint.
items.tweetA: 21x faster forward pass at 512K context. Training-only — the deployed model runs vanilla attention with zero overhead. Nous Research's Lighthouse Attention removes the usual tradeoff: you get the training speedup without any inference penalty. If it scales, this is how you train long-context models cheaply.
items.tweetB: The efficient attention holy grail: speed during training, nothing changes at deployment. Lighthouse Attention reports 21x latency reduction at 512K context and lower final loss. Most efficient-attention papers don't get adopted because of deployment friction. This one removes it.

items.title: Google DeepMind AI Co-Mathematician: 48% on FrontierMath Tier 4 (new high), solved open problems in active research sessions
items.idea: Google DeepMind's AI Co-Mathematician is an async, stateful, multi-workstream research workbench for mathematicians. It reaches 48% on FrontierMath Tier 4 — a new high for AI systems on the hardest tier of the hardest math benchmark. Early applications produced solved open problems, fresh research directions, and recovered overlooked citations during active mathematical research sessions. The workbench design (async, stateful, multi-workstream) generalizes to any expert workflow where sessions span days rather than minutes.
items.angle: The 'when does AI contribute to original scientific research' question now has a concrete data point. 48% on Tier 4 FrontierMath + solved open problems in active sessions puts the timeline for AI as genuine co-investigator in hard sciences in months, not years.
items.tweetA: Google DeepMind's AI Co-Mathematician: 48% on FrontierMath Tier 4 — the hardest AI math benchmark, new high. Also solved open problems and found overlooked citations in active research sessions. Two years ago this was science fiction. The AI-contributes-to-original-math timeline is no longer speculative.
items.tweetB: The interesting part of DeepMind's AI Co-Mathematician isn't the 48% benchmark score. It's the design: async, stateful, multi-workstream. Long-running computations and literature searches run in the background while the mathematician steers. This is what AI-assisted science looks like operationally — not a chatbot, a persistent workbench.

items.title: Memory Curse in LLM Agents: long histories degraded cooperation in 18/28 model-game combinations — forward-looking intent erodes, not suspicion
items.idea: Study across 7 LLMs and 4 social dilemma games over 500 rounds: expanding accessible history degraded cooperative behavior in 18/28 model-game combinations. The mechanism is not increased suspicion — it's erosion of forward-looking intent. Models reason about past interactions instead of future payoffs. Memory sanitization (swapping in synthetic cooperative records) restores cooperation — content matters more than length. Explicit chain-of-thought amplifies the collapse. A LoRA adapter trained only on forward-looking traces mitigates decay and transfers zero-shot to new games.
items.angle: This is a live production problem for anyone building long-running agentic systems. The fix is history curation, not context reduction. What you put in the history window matters more than how much you put in.
items.tweetA: Give an LLM agent more history and it gets worse, not better. In 18/28 model-game combinations, longer history degraded cooperative behavior. The mechanism: forward-looking intent erodes, the model thinks about the past instead of the future. Chain-of-thought amplifies it. This is a live production problem.
items.tweetB: Memory sanitization restores LLM agent cooperation better than shortening context. The Memory Curse is about content not length — replace bad history with synthetic cooperative records and behavior resets. What you put in the history window matters more than how much you put in.

items.title: Is Grep All You Need? Harness design accounts for most of the performance gap attributed to vector embeddings in coding agents
items.idea: New paper evaluates grep-style text search against embedding-based retrieval in coding agents under controlled conditions. Grep matches or exceeds embedding retrieval on coding tasks. The key finding: harness design accounts for most of the performance differential typically attributed to retrieval method. Holding retrieval constant and varying the harness produces larger performance shifts than holding the harness constant and varying retrieval. Prior retrieval comparisons were confounded by harness differences.
items.angle: Vector databases are the confident default in production coding agent stacks. This paper says: wrong default. Fix the harness before you upgrade the index. The yield is higher and the cost is lower.
items.tweetA: Grep beats vector embeddings in coding agents when you hold the harness constant. The performance gap everyone attributed to 'better retrieval' was actually harness design. You might be running Pinecone on a problem that grep solves. Simpler stack, same output.
items.tweetB: Most important coding agent paper this week: it's not the retrieval method, it's the harness. Vector databases are the confident default in agentic stacks. This paper says: confident, wrong. Fix the harness before you upgrade the index — the yield is higher and the cost is lower.

items.title: AEvo: meta-agent edits the procedure, not just the candidate — 26% gain over evolution baseline via accumulated search logs
items.idea: AEvo separates self-improvement into two roles: a candidate-proposer that generates the next attempt, and a meta-agent that observes traces and edits the procedure used to propose future candidates. Past runs — candidates, feedback, traces, failures — function as memory the meta-agent reads when revising the procedure. Reports 26% relative gain over the strongest evolution baseline on agentic and reasoning benchmarks, SOTA on three open-ended optimization tasks. The key insight: accumulated search logs are training signal for procedure-level updates.
items.angle: The procedure-editing meta-agent is the right abstraction for compound agent improvement. The logs every current production agent system discards after each run are the most valuable training signal available. AEvo shows how to use them.
items.tweetA: AEvo: separate the agent that proposes candidates from the meta-agent that edits how candidates get proposed. Feed past failures back to the meta-agent as memory. 26% gain over the strongest evolution baseline. The logs you're throwing away after each run might be your best training signal.
items.tweetB: The difference between an agent that improves slowly and one that compounds: a meta-agent that reads traces and edits procedures. AEvo operationalizes accumulated search history into procedure-level updates — 26% above the best evolution baseline. This is the architecture paper for agent self-improvement in 2026.
